sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A biochemist studies cellular respiration by examining a galvanic (voltaic) cell that models the thermodynamics of the electron transport chain. The cell consists of a NADH/NAD⁺ half-cell (E° = −0.32 V) and an O₂/H₂O half-cell (E° = +0.82 V). Electrons flow spontaneously from NADH through the electron transport chain to oxygen. The biochemist calculates the standard cell potential and uses it to determine the free energy available to drive ATP synthesis.
questions.stem: The standard cell potential (E°_cell) for the spontaneous transfer of electrons from NADH to O₂ is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: +1.14 V, calculated as E°_cathode − E°_anode = (+0.82) − (−0.32) = +1.14 V

choices.label: B
choices.text: −1.14 V, indicating a non-spontaneous reaction

choices.label: C
choices.text: +0.50 V, calculated as the average of the two half-cell potentials

choices.label: D
choices.text: −0.50 V, calculated as E°_anode − E°_cathode

explanations.A: Correct. E°_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode. The cathode is where reduction occurs — oxygen is reduced (O₂ → H₂O, E° = +0.82 V). The anode is where oxidation occurs — NADH is oxidized to NAD⁺ (E° = −0.32 V for the reduction half-reaction). E°_cell = (+0.82) − (−0.32) = +1.14 V. A positive E°_cell confirms spontaneity (ΔG° = −nFE°_cell < 0). This 1.14 V represents the thermodynamic driving force for electron transfer from NADH to oxygen — sufficient to pump enough protons to synthesize approximately 2.5 ATP per NADH.
explanations.B: A negative cell potential would indicate a non-spontaneous reaction requiring energy input. However, NADH oxidation by O₂ is highly exergonic and spontaneous under physiological conditions — it is the thermodynamic basis of all aerobic metabolism. The reaction proceeds because NADH (a strong reductant) is paired with O₂ (a strong oxidant), giving E°_cell = +1.14 V.
explanations.C: 0.50 V is the arithmetic mean of the two half-cell potentials [(+0.82 + (−0.32))/2]. Cell potential is not calculated by averaging. E°_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode accounts for the fact that one half-reaction is reversed (oxidation at the anode).
explanations.D: −0.50 V is the negative of the average — doubly incorrect. E°_cell requires identifying which electrode is the cathode (higher reduction potential, +0.82 V) and which is the anode (lower reduction potential, −0.32 V), then subtracting: (+0.82) − (−0.32) = +1.14 V.

questions.stem: The standard free energy change (ΔG°) for the transfer of 2 moles of electrons from NADH to O₂ (E°_cell = +1.14 V, F = 96,500 C/mol) is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: −220 kJ/mol, calculated from ΔG° = −nFE°_cell = −(2)(96,500)(1.14)

choices.label: B
choices.text: +220 kJ/mol, indicating energy input is required

choices.label: C
choices.text: −1.14 kJ/mol, because ΔG° = −E°_cell in kilojoules

choices.label: D
choices.text: 0 kJ/mol, because the reaction is at equilibrium

explanations.A: Correct. ΔG° = −nFE°_cell = −(2 mol)(96,500 C/mol)(1.14 V) = −220,020 J/mol ≈ −220 kJ/mol. The negative sign confirms the reaction is exergonic and spontaneous. For comparison, hydrolysis of one ATP releases ~30 kJ/mol, so −220 kJ/mol is thermodynamically sufficient to drive synthesis of multiple ATP molecules. (In practice, approximately 2.5 ATP are synthesized per NADH via chemiosmosis, with the rest dissipated as heat.)
explanations.B: +220 kJ/mol would indicate a non-spontaneous reaction requiring energy input (like electrolysis). The NADH oxidation by O₂ is the most exergonic reaction in metabolism — it is the thermodynamic source of energy for all aerobic ATP production. A positive ΔG° contradicts both the positive E°_cell and the biological reality.
explanations.C: ΔG° does not equal −E°_cell in kilojoules. The relationship is ΔG° = −nFE°_cell, where n (number of moles of electrons) and F (Faraday's constant, 96,500 C/mol) are essential multiplying factors. Without n and F, the units don't balance (volts ≠ kJ/mol).
explanations.D: ΔG° = 0 describes equilibrium. Far from equilibrium, the actual ΔG (not ΔG°) equals zero when the reaction has run to completion and Q = K. The standard free energy change of −220 kJ/mol indicates a highly exergonic reaction far from equilibrium under standard conditions.

questions.stem: In oxidation-reduction reactions, the oxidizing agent is the species that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: loses electrons and increases in oxidation number

choices.label: B
choices.text: neither gains nor loses electrons but facilitates the transfer

choices.label: C
choices.text: gains electrons and decreases in oxidation number, causing the other reactant to be oxidized

choices.label: D
choices.text: always contains oxygen in its chemical formula

explanations.A: This describes the reducing agent — the species that loses electrons (is oxidized) and increases in oxidation number. The reducing agent donates electrons to the oxidizing agent.
explanations.B: A catalyst facilitates reactions without being consumed and without gaining or losing electrons. The oxidizing agent is actively consumed in the redox reaction — it gains electrons and is chemically transformed to a reduced product.
explanations.C: Correct. The oxidizing agent accepts electrons from the reducing agent. By accepting electrons, the oxidizing agent's oxidation number decreases (it is reduced). The mnemonic 'OIL RIG' helps: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). The oxidizing agent causes the other reactant to be oxidized (hence its name) while being reduced itself. Common oxidizing agents include O₂, F₂, H₂O₂, MnO₄⁻, Cr₂O₇²⁻, and NAD⁺ in biological systems.
explanations.D: Many powerful oxidizing agents contain oxygen (O₂, H₂O₂, MnO₄⁻, HNO₃), but not all do. F₂ is the strongest oxidizing agent and contains no oxygen. Conversely, some oxygen-containing compounds are reducing agents (e.g., oxalate, C₂O₄²⁻). Oxidizing power is determined by reduction potential, not by the presence of oxygen in the formula.

questions.stem: In a galvanic cell, oxidation occurs at the anode and reduction occurs at the cathode. Conventional current flows:

choices.label: A
choices.text: from anode to cathode through the external circuit, in the same direction as electron flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: from anode to cathode through the salt bridge and from cathode to anode through the external circuit

choices.label: C
choices.text: only through the salt bridge, not through the external circuit

choices.label: D
choices.text: from cathode to anode through the external circuit, opposite to electron flow (electrons flow anode→cathode; conventional current flows cathode→anode)

explanations.A: Electrons flow from anode (oxidation, higher energy electrons) to cathode (reduction) through the external circuit. Conventional current (the historical convention of positive charge flow) is defined as flowing in the opposite direction of electron flow. So if electrons flow anode→cathode, conventional current flows cathode→anode in the external circuit.
explanations.B: This reverses both pathways. Electrons flow through the external circuit (from anode to cathode), not through the salt bridge. The salt bridge allows ion migration between half-cells — cations flow toward the cathode half-cell and anions toward the anode half-cell to maintain electrical neutrality.
explanations.C: The salt bridge carries ionic current between the two half-cells (completing the circuit inside the cell) but conventional current also flows through the external circuit (wire/load). Both pathways are necessary — the salt bridge prevents charge buildup in the half-cells while the external circuit allows electrons to flow and do electrical work.
explanations.D: Correct. In a galvanic cell: electrons flow from anode → external circuit → cathode (negative to positive terminal). Conventional current (defined as the direction positive charges would flow) is opposite to electron flow: cathode → external circuit → anode. This is the standard convention used in circuit analysis, even though actual charge carriers (electrons in metals) flow the other way. In the electrolyte (including the salt bridge), current is carried by ions: cations migrate toward the cathode and anions migrate toward the anode.

questions.stem: The Nernst equation (E = E° − (RT/nF)lnQ) describes how cell potential changes with concentration. For a galvanic cell at 25°C, increasing the concentration of the oxidizing agent (at the cathode) would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: decrease the cell potential, because higher concentration means more product

choices.label: B
choices.text: cause the reaction to reverse direction spontaneously

choices.label: C
choices.text: have no effect, because cell potential depends only on E° and temperature

choices.label: D
choices.text: increase the cell potential, because higher [oxidizing agent] drives the reaction further from equilibrium, increasing the thermodynamic driving force

explanations.A: Increasing the concentration of the oxidizing agent (a reactant in the overall cell reaction) would decrease Q (the reaction quotient). The Nernst equation shows E = E° − (RT/nF)lnQ — when Q decreases, lnQ becomes more negative, so the subtracted term becomes less negative (or positive), increasing E. Decreasing the oxidant concentration would decrease the cell potential.
explanations.B: The reaction reverses only when E becomes negative (Q > K, beyond equilibrium). Increasing the oxidizing agent concentration makes Q smaller, pushing the reaction further from equilibrium in the forward direction — the opposite of reversal.
explanations.C: Cell potential depends on both E° and the reaction quotient Q. The Nernst equation specifically shows how E deviates from E° based on concentrations (through Q). Only at standard conditions (all concentrations = 1 M, gases at 1 atm) does E = E°.
explanations.D: Correct. Increasing [oxidizing agent] drives Q lower (it's a reactant). From the Nernst equation, lower Q gives higher E. Intuitively, having more oxidant available increases the thermodynamic drive for the reduction half-reaction at the cathode, raising the cell potential. Conversely, increasing product concentration or decreasing reactant concentration would lower E toward zero (equilibrium). This principle is exploited in concentration cells.

questions.stem: Electrolysis uses electrical energy to drive non-spontaneous reactions. In the electrolysis of water (2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂), which species is produced at the cathode?

choices.label: A
choices.text: O₂, because oxygen is the primary product of water electrolysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: H₂, because reduction occurs at the cathode — H⁺ ions gain electrons to form H₂ gas

choices.label: C
choices.text: H₂O₂, because water partially oxidizes at the cathode

choices.label: D
choices.text: Na⁺ ions, because metal cations always migrate to the cathode

explanations.A: O₂ is produced at the anode in water electrolysis, not the cathode. At the anode, oxidation occurs: 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻. The cathode is where reduction occurs, producing H₂.
explanations.B: Correct. The cathode in electrolysis is where reduction occurs. In water electrolysis: 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂ (at the cathode). The cathode is connected to the negative terminal of the power supply, attracting cations (H⁺) and supplying electrons for reduction. O₂ is produced at the anode (oxidation: 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻). The anode is connected to the positive terminal. Mnemonic for electrolytic cells: RED CAT, AN OX (Reduction at Cathode, Anode is Oxidation) — the same convention as galvanic cells.
explanations.C: H₂O₂ is not a product of water electrolysis under normal conditions. At the cathode, water or H⁺ ions are reduced to hydrogen gas (H₂), not to hydrogen peroxide.
explanations.D: Na⁺ ions are not present in pure water electrolysis. Even in solutions containing Na⁺ (like NaCl solution), Na⁺ is not reduced at the cathode under aqueous conditions because water is preferentially reduced instead (H₂ is produced rather than Na metal, which would react violently with water).

questions.stem: Balancing a redox reaction in acidic solution requires the use of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: only H₂ molecules to balance hydrogen atoms

choices.label: B
choices.text: H₂O to balance oxygen atoms, H⁺ to balance hydrogen atoms, and electrons to balance charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: OH⁻ ions to balance oxygen, which converts the reaction from acidic to basic conditions

choices.label: D
choices.text: only electrons — all other atoms balance automatically in redox reactions

explanations.A: H₂ molecules are not used in balancing redox reactions — they would be a product or reactant if hydrogen gas is involved, not a balancing species. In acidic conditions, H⁺ ions (not H₂) are used to balance hydrogen atoms after oxygen has been balanced with water.
explanations.B: Correct. The half-reaction method in acidic solution follows this sequence: (1) balance all atoms except O and H; (2) add H₂O to balance oxygen; (3) add H⁺ to balance hydrogen; (4) add electrons to balance charge. For example, balancing MnO₄⁻ → Mn²⁺: Add 4H₂O to balance O, add 8H⁺ to balance H, add 5e⁻ to balance charge: MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O.
explanations.C: Using OH⁻ to balance oxygen is the method for balancing in basic (not acidic) conditions. In basic solution, OH⁻ is a major species and can be used as a balancing species. Using OH⁻ in acidic conditions would artificially introduce hydroxide to a solution where it is not the relevant species.
explanations.D: Atoms do not balance automatically — they must be explicitly balanced for each element. Electrons balance the net charge, but oxygen and hydrogen must be balanced with H₂O and H⁺ (in acid) or H₂O and OH⁻ (in base) before electrons are assigned.

questions.stem: Faraday's law of electrolysis states that the mass of substance deposited at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed. If 2 moles of electrons are passed through a CuSO₄ solution, the mass of copper deposited at the cathode is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 63.5 g, because Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu requires 2 moles of electrons per mole of copper, and the molar mass of Cu is 63.5 g/mol

choices.label: B
choices.text: 127 g, because each electron deposits one atom of copper

choices.label: C
choices.text: 31.75 g, because only half the electrons are effective

choices.label: D
choices.text: 0 g, because copper cannot be deposited from an aqueous solution

explanations.A: Correct. The cathodic half-reaction is Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu⁰. This means 2 moles of electrons deposit 1 mole of copper metal. Molar mass of Cu = 63.5 g/mol. Mass deposited = 1 mol × 63.5 g/mol = 63.5 g. Faraday's law: m = (Q/F) × (M/n), where Q is charge passed (in coulombs), F = 96,500 C/mol e⁻, M is molar mass, and n is the number of electrons per mole of analyte. This principle underlies electroplating, electrorefining, and electrochemical analytical methods.
explanations.B: 127 g would be 2 moles of copper (2 × 63.5). This error arises from confusing 'moles of electrons' with 'moles of copper deposited.' Cu²⁺ requires 2 electrons per atom, so 2 moles of electrons deposit only 1 mole (not 2 moles) of copper.
explanations.C: 31.75 g is half a mole of copper (0.5 × 63.5). This would result from treating 2 moles of electrons as depositing 0.5 moles of copper — the ratio is inverted. 2e⁻ per Cu atom means 2 mol e⁻ deposits 1 mol Cu, not 0.5 mol.
explanations.D: Copper can definitely be deposited from aqueous CuSO₄ solution — this is the basis of copper electroplating. Cu²⁺ ions in solution are reduced to Cu metal at the cathode. Electroplating with copper from CuSO₄ solutions is a well-established industrial process.

questions.stem: Oxidation numbers help track electron transfer. In the reaction: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu, the oxidation number of zinc:

choices.label: A
choices.text: decreases from 0 to −2, indicating reduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: stays at 0 throughout, because zinc is a metal

choices.label: C
choices.text: increases from 0 to +2, indicating oxidation — zinc loses two electrons to become Zn²⁺

choices.label: D
choices.text: changes from +2 to 0, indicating reduction of the zinc ion

explanations.A: Decreasing oxidation number indicates reduction (gaining electrons). Zinc's oxidation number increases (from 0 to +2), indicating oxidation (losing electrons). This is the opposite of reduction.
explanations.B: Elemental zinc (Zn metal) has oxidation number 0, but it changes during the reaction. Zinc atoms lose 2 electrons to become Zn²⁺ ions in ZnSO₄ — its oxidation number increases from 0 to +2. Metals can have any oxidation number in compounds; they are 0 only in their elemental form.
explanations.C: Correct. In elemental zinc (Zn), the oxidation number is 0 (by convention, elements in their standard state have oxidation number 0). In ZnSO₄, zinc is Zn²⁺ (oxidation number +2). The increase from 0 to +2 represents loss of 2 electrons — oxidation. Zinc is the reducing agent; it reduces Cu²⁺ to Cu⁰ while being oxidized to Zn²⁺. This reaction forms the basis of the Daniell cell (galvanic cell).
explanations.D: A change from +2 to 0 describes what happens to copper in this reaction (Cu²⁺ → Cu⁰), not zinc. Copper is reduced (gains 2 electrons), while zinc is oxidized. This answer describes the copper half-reaction, not the zinc half-reaction.

questions.stem: A salt bridge in a galvanic cell maintains electrical neutrality by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: transferring electrons directly between the two half-cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: supplying additional reactants to the electrode surfaces

choices.label: C
choices.text: allowing ions to migrate between half-cells — anions migrate to the anode compartment and cations migrate to the cathode compartment — maintaining charge balance without mixing the electrode solutions

choices.label: D
choices.text: converting chemical energy directly to heat, preventing electrode polarization

explanations.A: Electrons flow through the external circuit (the wire connecting the electrodes), not through the salt bridge. The salt bridge carries ionic current, not electronic current. Allowing electrons to pass through the salt bridge would short-circuit the cell by bypassing the external circuit and the load.
explanations.B: The salt bridge contains an inert electrolyte (KCl, KNO₃, agar gel) that does not participate in the electrode reactions. It does not supply reactants — it only provides an ionic conducting path between the half-cells.
explanations.C: Correct. As the cell operates, the anode compartment develops a positive charge buildup (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; Zn²⁺ accumulates) and the cathode compartment develops a negative charge buildup (Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu; Cu²⁺ is consumed). Without a salt bridge, this charge imbalance would create an electric field opposing further electron flow, and the cell would stop. The salt bridge allows anions (Cl⁻ or NO₃⁻) to flow into the anode compartment (neutralizing Zn²⁺ buildup) and cations (K⁺) to flow into the cathode compartment (replacing consumed Cu²⁺). This maintains electroneutrality in both compartments, allowing continuous current flow.
explanations.D: The salt bridge does not convert chemical energy to heat — it is an ionic conductor, not a resistive heater. Electrode polarization is prevented by maintaining ionic balance through ion migration, not through thermal effects.
